news_summary: NASA’s Hubble captured a rare and exciting real-time event of a comet breaking apart, showcasing the dynamic nature of our solar system.
news_summary_fr: Le télescope Hubble de la NASA a capturé en temps réel un événement rare et fascinant : la désintégration d'une comète, illustrant ainsi la nature dynamique de notre système solaire.
news_summary_es: El telescopio Hubble de la NASA captó un fenómeno excepcional y apasionante en tiempo real: la desintegración de un cometa, lo que pone de manifiesto la naturaleza dinámica de nuestro sistema solar.

articles.title: NASA’s Hubble accidentally caught a comet breaking apart in real time
articles.summary: In an incredibly lucky cosmic accident, NASA’s Hubble Space Telescope captured a comet breaking apart in real time—something astronomers have long tried and failed to observe. The comet, C/2025 K1 (ATLAS), wasn’t even the original target, but when researchers pivoted to it, they unknowingly caught it mid-disintegration into multiple pieces.
articles.content:
In a remarkable stroke of luck, NASA's Hubble Space Telescope observed a comet in the middle of breaking apart. The odds of witnessing such an event at exactly the right moment are extremely low. The findings were published in the journal Icarus.

The comet K1, formally known as C/2025 K1 (ATLAS) -- not to be confused with interstellar comet 3I/ATLAS -- was not the intended focus of the Hubble observations.

"Sometimes the best science happens by accident," said co-investigator John Noonan, a research professor in the Department of Physics at Auburn University in Alabama. "This comet got observed because our original comet was not viewable due to some new technical constraints after we won our proposal. We had to find a new target -- and right when we observed it, it happened to break apart, which is the slimmest of slim chances."

A Surprise Discovery in Hubble Data

Noonan did not realize the comet was breaking up until he reviewed the images the following day. "While I was taking an initial look at the data, I saw that there were four comets in those images when we only proposed to look at one," said Noonan. "So we knew this was something really, really special."

Capturing a comet in the act of fragmenting is something the team had long hoped to achieve. They had submitted multiple proposals to observe such an event with Hubble, but timing these observations has proven extremely difficult, and previous attempts had not succeeded.

"The irony is now we're just studying a regular comet and it crumbles in front of our eyes," said principal investigator Dennis Bodewits, also a professor in Auburn University's Department of Physics.

"Comets are leftovers of the era of solar system formation, so they're made of 'old stuff' -- the primordial materials that made our solar system," said Bodewits. "But they are not pristine -- they've been heated; they've been irradiated by the Sun and by cosmic rays. So, when looking at a comet's composition, the question we always have is, 'Is this a primitive property or is this due to evolution?' By cracking open a comet, you can see the ancient material that has not been processed."

Hubble Reveals Comet K1 Splitting Into Pieces

Hubble observed K1 breaking into at least four separate fragments, each surrounded by its own coma, the cloud of gas and dust that forms around a comet's icy core. While Hubble clearly resolved these pieces, ground-based telescopes could only detect them as faint, barely separated points of light.

The images were taken about a month after the comet's closest approach to the Sun, known as perihelion. At that point, K1 had traveled inside Mercury's orbit, roughly one-third of the distance between Earth and the Sun. This is when comets experience the most intense heat and stress. Many long-period comets, including K1, tend to begin breaking apart shortly after this phase.

Timing the Breakup and Tracking the Fragments

Before it began to disintegrate, K1 was likely slightly larger than a typical comet, measuring about 5 miles across. Researchers estimate the breakup started around eight days before Hubble captured it. The telescope recorded three 20-second images taken on consecutive days from Nov. 8 through Nov. 10, 2025. During that short window, one of the smaller fragments also split further.

Thanks to Hubble's high resolution, scientists were able to trace the fragments backward to their original state as a single object. This allowed them to reconstruct the sequence of events. However, their analysis revealed an unexpected puzzle. Why was there a delay between the breakup and the bright outbursts later seen from Earth? If fresh ice was exposed, why did the comet not brighten right away?

A New Mystery About Comet Brightness

The team has proposed several possible explanations. A comet's brightness is largely due to sunlight reflecting off dust particles. When a comet first breaks open, it exposes clean ice rather than dust. One possibility is that a dry dust layer must first form and then be blown off. Another idea is that heat penetrates beneath the surface, builds pressure, and eventually releases a shell of dust into space.

"Never before has Hubble caught a fragmenting comet this close to when it actually fell apart. Most of the time, it's a few weeks to a month later. And in this case, we were able to see it just days after," said Noonan. "This is telling us something very important about the physics of what's happening at the comet's surface. We may be seeing the timescale it takes to form a substantial dust layer that can then be ejected by the gas."

Strange Chemistry and Future Insights

The research team plans to continue analyzing gases released by the comet. Early observations from ground-based telescopes suggest that K1 has an unusual chemical makeup, showing significantly lower levels of carbon compared to most comets. Additional data from Hubble's STIS (Space Telescope Imaging Spectrograph) and COS (Cosmic Origins Spectrograph) instruments are expected to provide deeper insight into its composition and what it can reveal about the origins of the solar system.

A Comet That Will Not Return

K1 is now a cluster of fragments located about 250 million miles from Earth. It can be found in the constellation Pisces and is moving away from the Sun, likely never to return to the inner solar system.

The Hubble Space Telescope has been in operation for more than 30 years and continues to deliver important discoveries that expand our understanding of the universe. It is a joint project between NASA and ESA (European Space Agency). NASA's Goddard Space Flight Center in Greenbelt, Maryland, oversees the mission, with support from Lockheed Martin Space in Denver. Scientific operations are conducted by the Space Telescope Science Institute in Baltimore, which is run by the Association of Universities for Research in Astronomy.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a rare and significant scientific observation by NASA's Hubble Space Telescope capturing a comet breaking apart in real time, providing valuable insights into comet composition and solar system formation. This discovery has broad scientific importance and advances understanding of cosmic phenomena, fulfilling criteria for positive real-world impact, significance, and substance.
articles.personality_title: Hubble telescope captures rare moment of comet breaking apart
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists have long wanted to see a comet break apart in real time, but it’s very hard to catch because it happens suddenly and far away in space. Comets are icy objects from the early solar system, and studying them helps us learn about how our solar system formed.

**What happened** – NASA’s Hubble Space Telescope accidentally captured images of a comet called C/2025 K1 (ATLAS) breaking into at least four pieces. This happened in November 2025, shortly after the comet passed very close to the Sun. The comet wasn’t the original target of the observations, but when scientists switched to it, they caught it mid-breakup.

**Impact** – This is one of the first times astronomers have seen a comet split apart so clearly and so soon after the breakup started. The images show details that ground telescopes can’t see, like separate clouds of dust and gas around each fragment. This helps scientists understand what comets are made of and how they change when heated by the Sun.

**What’s next step** – Researchers will study the gases released by the comet using special instruments on Hubble. They want to learn why the comet’s brightness changed days after it broke apart and why it has unusual chemicals compared to other comets. This information could reveal more about the early solar system.

**One-sentence takeaway** – By capturing a comet breaking apart in real time, Hubble gave scientists a rare chance to study the building blocks of our solar system up close.
articles.personality_title_fr: Le télescope Hubble capture un moment rare d’une comète se désintégrant
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les scientifiques cherchent depuis longtemps à observer une comète se briser en temps réel, mais c’est très difficile car cela se produit soudainement et très loin dans l’espace. Les comètes sont des corps glacés provenant des débuts du système solaire, et les étudier aide à comprendre sa formation.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Le télescope spatial Hubble de la NASA a accidentellement pris des images d’une comète appelée C/2025 K1 (ATLAS) se divisant en au moins quatre morceaux. Cela s’est produit en novembre 2025, peu après que la comète soit passée très près du Soleil. Ce n’était pas la cible initiale des observations, mais en changeant de cible, les scientifiques ont surpris la fragmentation.

**Impact** – C’est l’une des premières fois que les astronomes voient une comète se fendre aussi clairement et peu de temps après le début de la fragmentation. Les images montrent des détails invisibles depuis la Terre, comme des nuages de poussière et de gaz autour de chaque fragment. Cela aide à mieux comprendre la composition des comètes et leur évolution sous l’effet du Soleil.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs vont étudier les gaz émis par la comète grâce à des instruments spéciaux d’Hubble. Ils veulent comprendre pourquoi la luminosité a changé quelques jours après la rupture et pourquoi la comète a une composition chimique différente des autres. Ces données pourraient en apprendre plus sur les origines du système solaire.

**Résumé en une phrase** – En capturant une comète se désintégrant en temps réel, Hubble a offert aux scientifiques une occasion rare d’étudier de près les éléments constitutifs de notre système solaire.
articles.personality_title_es: El telescopio Hubble captura un raro momento de una cometa rompiéndose
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los científicos han querido durante mucho tiempo ver una cometa romperse en tiempo real, pero es muy difícil porque ocurre de repente y muy lejos en el espacio. Las cometas son objetos helados del sistema solar temprano, y estudiarlas ayuda a entender cómo se formó nuestro sistema solar.

**Qué pasó** – El telescopio espacial Hubble de la NASA capturó por accidente imágenes de una cometa llamada C/2025 K1 (ATLAS) que se rompió en al menos cuatro pedazos. Esto ocurrió en noviembre de 2025, poco después de que la cometa pasara muy cerca del Sol. La cometa no era el objetivo original, pero cuando cambiaron de objetivo, la captaron justo cuando se estaba fragmentando.

**Impacto** – Esta es una de las primeras veces que los astrónomos ven una cometa dividirse tan claramente y poco después de que empezara a romperse. Las imágenes muestran detalles que los telescopios en la Tierra no pueden ver, como nubes de polvo y gas alrededor de cada fragmento. Esto ayuda a los científicos a entender de qué están hechas las cometas y cómo cambian cuando el Sol las calienta.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores estudiarán los gases que la cometa libera usando instrumentos especiales en Hubble. Quieren saber por qué el brillo de la cometa cambió días después de romperse y por qué tiene una química diferente a otras cometas. Esta información podría revelar más sobre el sistema solar temprano.

**Frase clave** – Al capturar una cometa rompiéndose en tiempo real, Hubble dio a los científicos una oportunidad rara para estudiar de cerca los bloques de construcción de nuestro sistema solar.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a glowing comet gently fracturing into four luminous icy fragments, each surrounded by a soft halo of misty gas and dust, drifting apart against a deep space backdrop subtly hinting at the constellation Pisces, with a nearby stylized telescope silhouette observing the scene from a distance.
